title: Monero
description: Monero is a alternative L1 blockchain focused exclusively on private transactions by default. It uses Ring signatures and stealth addresses under the hood to make the system secure, private and decentralized

properties.name: Confidentiality
properties.notes: Stealth addresses are used so there is no way to get a user's total balance because each transaction is send to a fresh stealth address. Ring Confidential Transactions (RingCT) hide transaction amounts using commitments and range proofs. More info about RingCT here: https://www.getmonero.org/resources/moneropedia/ringCT.html

properties.name: Anonymity
properties.notes: Ring signatures allows senders to hide themselves in a ring group of users. When a transaction is emitted, the sender signs the data using ring signatures (group signatures) so that the verification can guarantee the transaction is coming from a group member but does not know who

properties.name: Asset privacy
properties.notes: You can only send XMR (native token) in Monero. There is no support for custom tokens like ERC20s or similar. Therefore there is no asset privacy because every transaction in Monero shows that XMR is being moved.

properties.name: Plausible deniability
properties.notes: Every transaction output has plausible deniability because the transaction uses a ring signature. All members of the group could deny sending that transaction and there will be no way to link the real sender to a specific group member.

properties.name: Number of secrets
properties.notes: A Monero account requires 2 secrets in order to work properly. A private spending key (secret #1) that will be used to authorize transactions from that account. A private viewing key that can be used to reveal the account's transactions. Both keys are computing using asymmetric cryptography (public and private keys).

properties.name: Time-to-finality
properties.notes: Monero is an alternative L1 blockchain with a 2-minute target block time. Funds are considered spendable (effectively final) after 10 confirmations, giving a time to finality of approximately 20 minutes (10 × 2 min).

properties.name: Deposit time
properties.notes: No mandatory deposit waiting period. Incoming funds are visible in the wallet after the first confirmation (~2 minutes) and fully spendable after 10 confirmations, which is captured in Time-to-finality.

properties.name: Withdraw time
properties.notes: No mandatory withdrawal waiting period enforced by the protocol beyond the standard confirmation time captured in Time-to-finality.

properties.name: Censorship resistance
properties.notes: The privacy by-default feature hides senders and receivers interactions in every transaction. This privacy-by-default property and the Proof of Work consensus mechanism make Monero censorship-resistant. Anyone can setup a Monero node with modest hardware (GPU optimized hardware does not work with Monero consensus algorithm) and start validating private-by-default transactions.

properties.name: External network dependence
properties.notes: Monero uses Proof of Work which requires miners to join the network and validate transactions. The POoW algorithm is ASIC resistant, meaning that Monero miners must use consumer-grade hardware for an efficient mining process.

properties.name: Escape hatch
properties.notes: Users can withdraw funds from Monero to exchanges or other blockchains at any time. Exchanges could block Monero transactions depending on regulations and compliance. The challenge for users is to find a trustworthy withdrawal method (e.g. bridge or centralized exchange) that would not flag their account just because of the interaction with Monero.

properties.name: Upgradeability
properties.notes: The Monero community pushes changes and upgrades to the Monero Blockchain. These changes are implemented by different teams depending on the specifics (core team, wallet team, development team, etc)

properties.name: Client-side proving
properties.notes: Monero wallets compute all the required data and proofs on the client devices. There is no external proving service available. There are multiple wallet options to chose from: GUI, CLI, mobile and hardware wallets.

properties.name: Third-party inspectability
properties.notes: Only the user has access to his balances and received transactions. The user can willingly share his viewing keys with any third-parties for compliance or audit purposes.

properties.name: Implementation maturity
properties.notes: Monero was launched in April 2014. It was a fair, pre-announced launch of the CryptoNote reference code. It was being upgrade since then by community proposals.

properties.name: Post-quantum secure
properties.notes: Monero cryptography is based on Elliptic Curve Cryptography therefore it is vulnerable to the Shor's algorithm in future feasible quantum computers.

properties.name: Layer of enforcement
properties.notes: There is no compliance enforcement mechanism in Monero blockchain. Some centralized exchanges can block Monero transactions due to national regulations or policies.

properties.name: Enforcement entities
properties.notes: There is no compliance enforcement mechanism in the Monero blockchain. This is one of the main selling points of the blockchain itself. Even though there is no control at the protocol layer, centralized exchanges can flag or block users using Monero.

properties.name: Type of compliance
properties.notes: The viewing keys were a selective disclosure mechanism for Monero users. According to their own documentation: outgoing transactions cannot be reliably viewed as of June 2017. Therefore, the balance of a Monero address as shown via a viewkey should not be relied upon.

properties.name: Point of enforcement
properties.notes: Some centralized exchanges or bridges to other L1s do not accept Monero transactions. This is not enforced in the Monero blockchain itself but in external third-parties due to government regulations or internal terms of services.

properties.name: Selective disclosure: viewing entity
properties.notes: A user could share his viewing key with a specific third-party for compliance or tax purposes. The third-party would only be able to read the user transaction's detail and transaction history. The third-party would not be able to authorize and send transaction in the Monero blockchain by using the viewing key.

properties.name: Selective disclosure: viewing control
properties.notes: There is no programable viewing control. The viewing keys setup is pre-defined by the protocol itself and the user is the only one who can share his viewing key to an authorized party.

properties.name: Cryptographic verifiability
properties.notes: Monero uses a wide variety of cryptographic primitives for various use cases. Comparing to altcoins, Monero cryptography is considered conservative, sound and robust. Comparing to Bitcoin, Monero uses much more primitives, and some of them are more advanced, especially those related to privacy and Proof of Work.

properties.name: Open source
properties.notes: Blockchain nodes code, protocol documentation, wallet interface and more repositories can be found at the Moreno project Github organization.

properties.name: Scalable state
properties.notes: Monero has private key images as nullifiers to prevent double spending attempts. So in every Monero transaction, a new set of private key images is added to the protocol state. The state can grow indefinitely depending on the usage.

properties.name: Client-side indexing
properties.notes: Stealth addresses make every transaction land on a fresh new address that is secretly controlled by a Monero account. In order for wallets to show the account balance, the application needs to be constantly scanning the network for transactions related to its corresponding stealth addresses

properties.name: Private state model
properties.notes: Every transaction on Monero has inputs (previously received transactions to a controlled stealth address) and outputs (transactions going to fresh new stealth addresses controlled by the receivers)

properties.name: On-chain storage
properties.notes: All data is stored on Monero blockchain itself. There is no concept of smart contracts but this option is the most appropriated because it represents saving state directly on-chain without depending on external networks or more complicated scanning mechanisms for emitted events.

properties.name: Access to DeFi
properties.notes: There is no DeFi ecosystem in Monero due to its payment-only nature. Multiple L1s have launched XMR bridges that generate a XMR token representation (wrapped XMR) in their respective L1s for their users to hold and use XMR (in some cases it could be used as collateral in lending platforms).

properties.name: Programmability / Generality
properties.notes: Monero lives in a payments only blockchain with no programmability features. There are no future plans to implement smart contract logic in Monero.
